# Concept Card: Deployments

## Definition
Transcript evidence থেকে derived canonical definition; conflicting views থাকলে সব variant রাখা হয়েছে।

## First Principles
System boundary, control loop, এবং failure behavior দৃষ্টিতে concept ব্যাখ্যা করতে হবে।

## Mental Model
এই concept-কে runtime contract হিসেবে ধরলে debugging ও design decisions সহজ হয়।

## Why It Exists
Operational complexity কমানো এবং reliable automation enable করা।

## Internal Working
Control plane state reconciliation + node-level execution pathway।

## YAML and Command Usage
Related raw commands এবং manifests reference-based রাখা হয়েছে; no rewrite of original evidence.

## Use Cases
Lab, production-like demos, and certification patterns থেকে mapped।

## Edge Cases
Version mismatch, policy conflict, scheduling/storage/network constraints উল্লেখ করা হয়েছে।

## Pitfalls
Warning/Pitfall entries evidence সহ retain করা হয়েছে।

## Troubleshooting Angle
Symptom -> Cause -> Fix logic chain evidence-based maintain করা হয়েছে।

## Exam Relevance
CKA/CKAD speed + correctness + troubleshooting expectations mapped।

## Source Contributions

### Evidence 95: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E85

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In case of replica set, a user input is required for this property, and it has to be written in the form of match labels as shown here.

### Evidence 98: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E88
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt

- Content: The match labels selector simply matches the labels specified under it to the labels on the pods.

### Evidence 135: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E125
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Well, there are multiple ways to do it.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 136: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E126
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The first is to update the number of replicas in the definition file to six.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 137: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E127
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Then run the kubectl replace command to specify the same file using the f parameter, and that will update the replica set to have six replicas.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 138: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E128
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The second way to do it is to run the kube control scale command.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 139: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E129
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Use the replicas parameter to provide the new number of replicas and specify the same file as input.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 140: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E130
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You may either input the definition file or provide the replica set name in the type name format.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 141: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E131
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, remember that using the file name as input will not result in the number of replicas being updated automatically in the file.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 142: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/026_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E132
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 026_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In other words, the number of replicas in the replica set definition file will still be three, even though you scaled your replica set to have six replicas using the Kube control scale command and the file as input.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

- Content: In this lecture we will discuss about Kubernetes deployments.

### Evidence 261: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For a minute.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 262: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let us forget about pods and replica sets and other Kubernetes concepts, and talk about how you might want to deploy your application in a production environment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 263: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say, for example, you have a web server that needs to be deployed in a production environment.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 264: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You need not one, but many such instances of the web server running for obvious reasons.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 265: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Secondly, whenever newer versions of application builds become available on the Docker registry, you would like to upgrade your Docker instances seamlessly.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 266: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, when you upgrade your instances, you do not want to upgrade all of them at once, as we just did.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 267: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This may impact users accessing our applications, so you might want to upgrade them one after the other.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 268: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And that kind of upgrade is known as rolling updates.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 269: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Suppose one of the upgrades you performed resulted in an unexpected error, and you're asked to undo the recent change You would like to be able to roll back the changes that were recently carried out.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 270: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Finally, say, for example, you would like to make multiple changes to your environment, such as upgrading the underlying web server versions, as well as scaling your environment and also modifying the resource allocations, etc. you do not want to apply each change immediately after the command is run.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 271: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Instead, you would like to apply a path to your environment, make the changes and then resume so that all the changes are rolled out together.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 272: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: All of these capabilities are available with the Kubernetes deployments.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 273: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So far in this course, we discussed about pods, which deploy single instances of our application, such as the web application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 274: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this case, each container is encapsulated in pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 275: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Multiple such pods are deployed using replication controllers or replica sets.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 276: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And then comes deployment, which is a Kubernetes object that comes higher in the hierarchy.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 277: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The deployment provides us with the capability to upgrade the underlying instances seamlessly using rolling updates, undo changes, and pause and resume changes as required.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 278: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E19
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So how do we create a deployment?
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

- Content: As with the previous components, we first create a deployment definition file.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The contents of the deployment definition file are exactly similar to the replica set definition file, except for the kind which is now going to be deployment.

### Evidence 282: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E23

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 284: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E25

### Evidence 285: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E26

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt

### Evidence 286: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E27

### Evidence 287: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E28

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 029_Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 288: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/029_Deployments.extraction.md::E29

### Evidence 389: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In the Kubernetes world, the imperative way of managing infrastructure is using commands like the kubectl run command to create a pod, the kubectl create deployment command to create a deployment, the kubectl expose command to create a service to expose a deployment, and the kubectl edit command may be used to edit an existing object.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Configuration

### Evidence 390: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For scaling a deployment or replica set, use the kubectl scale command, and updating the image on a deployment, we use the kubectl set image command.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 391: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E45
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, there are limited in functionality and will require forming long and complex commands for advanced use cases such as creating a multi-container pod or deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 439: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E1
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this lecture we will talk about updates and rollbacks in a deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 440: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Before we look at how we upgrade our application, let's try to understand rollouts and versioning in a deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 441: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When you first create a deployment it triggers a rollout.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 442: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A new rollout creates a new replica set, which is recorded as a new deployment revision in the future when the application is upgraded, meaning when the container version is updated to a new one, a new rollout is triggered and a new deployment revision is created, named revision two.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 443: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This helps us keep track of the changes made to our deployment, and enables us to roll back to a previous version of deployment if necessary.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 444: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You can see the status of your rollout by running the command kube control rollout status, followed by the name of the deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 445: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To see the revisions and history of rollout, run the Kube control rollout history command followed by the deployment name, and this will show you the revisions and history of our deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 446: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There are two types of deployment strategies.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 447: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say for example you have five replicas of your web application instance deployed.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 448: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One way to upgrade these to a newer version is to destroy all of these, and then create newer versions of application instances.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 449: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Meaning first destroy the five running instances and then deploy five new instances of the new application version.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 450: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The problem with this, as you can imagine, is that during the period after the older versions are down and before any newer version is up, the application is down and inaccessible to users.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 451: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This strategy is known as the recreate strategy, and thankfully this is not the default deployment strategy.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 452: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The second strategy is where we do not destroy all of them at once.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 453: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Instead, we take down the older version and bring up a newer version one by one.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 454: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This way the application never goes down and the upgrade is seamless.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 455: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Remember, if you do not specify a strategy while creating the deployment, it will assume it to be rolling.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 456: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Update.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 457: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E19
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In other words, rolling update is the default deployment strategy.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 458: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 459: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E21
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: How exactly do you update your deployment?
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 460: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When I say update it could be different things, such as updating your application version by updating the version of Docker containers used, updating their labels, or updating the number of replicas, etc. since we already have a deployment definition file, it is easy for us to modify this file once we make the necessary changes.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 461: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We run the kube control apply command to apply the changes.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 462: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A new rollout is triggered and a new revision of the deployment is created.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 463: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But there is another way to do the same thing.

### Evidence 464: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E26
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 05_Application Lifecycle Management / 096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.txt

### Evidence 465: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E27

### Evidence 466: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E28

### Evidence 472: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E34

### Evidence 473: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E35
- Type: Implementation Step

- Content: Let's look at how a deployment performs an upgrade under the hood when a new deployment is created, say, to deploy five replicas, it first creates a replica set automatically, which in turn creates the number of pods required to meet the number of replicas.

### Evidence 474: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/096_Rolling Updates and Rollbacks.extraction.md::E36

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When you upgrade your application, as we saw in the previous slide, the Kubernetes deployment object creates a new replica set under the hood and starts deploying the containers there.

### Evidence 781: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E94

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_ / 249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Cgroups is the Linux feature, the Linux primitive that allows whether you're working with Docker or Kubernetes, it allows you to specify resource limits on your containers.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, On-Prem

### Evidence 782: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/11_Install _Kubernetes the kubeadm way_/249_Demo - Deployment with Kubeadm.extraction.md::E95

- Content: So if you want to say this container can only use 512MB of Ram, it's using the Cgroups under the hood.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, On-Prem

- Content: Now, when you configure the Kubelet process and when you configure the the runtime, which in our case is container D, there's two different drivers that are available.

- Content: Now you can use whichever one you'd like, but you have to make sure that whichever one you select, both the Kubelet and your container runtime are configured to use the same Cgroup driver.

### Evidence 1052: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, even a relatively simple WordPress site might need the following, a deployment to deploy the pods that you wanna run, such as MySQL database servers or web servers, a persistent volume to store the database, a persistent volume claim, a service to expose the web server running in a pod to the internet, a secret to store credentials like admin passwords and other things, and maybe even more if you want extra stuff like periodic backups, jobs and so on.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1053: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, at least in multiple files, they'd be somewhat organized and we'd know we'll find deployment related stuff in the mydeployment.yaml file, for example.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1054: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It doesn't really know that this persistent volume and that deployment and that secret and that service are all part of a big application called WordPress.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1055: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E44
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For now, understand that Helm works as a package manager with Install or Uninstall Wizard, and also as a release manager helping us upgrade or rollback applications.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1056: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If there's a need, you can return to revision number 1 through a rollback.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1057: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E43
- Type: Command
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Helm 2 was less sophisticated when it came to how it did such rollbacks.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1058: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E44
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And when a rollback command is issued, Helm compares the current chart, which is the chart that has the WordPress image 5.8 in it with the previous chart, which is the chart that has a WordPress 4.8 image in it and realizes that they're different.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 1059: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E47
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say we install a WordPress deployment using a Helm chart, which creates revision 1 just like before.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1060: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E53
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Since there is only one revision, Helm does not detect any changes, and so it does not roll back or make any changes to the deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1061: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E54
- Type: Comparison
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So, Helm 2 compares the current chart with the previous chart to identify the difference between revisions to make a rollback decision.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1062: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E61
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, besides rollbacks, there are also things like upgrades to consider, where Helm 2 was also lacking.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

- Content: So what I like to do is if there's an issue with the deployment, I'm gonna start by doing a kubectl describe deployment, and we're gonna take a look at the deployment.

### Evidence 1224: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/17_Mock Exams/308_Mock Exam - 3 - Solution _ (Optional).extraction.md::E336
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 17_Mock Exams / 308_Mock Exam - 3 - Solution _ (Optional).txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So this is before we even got to the question, we could see that the controller told the replica set that was created by the deployment to go from zero to one, but we never got a message from the deployment controller to tell it to go from one to three.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1225: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/17_Mock Exams/308_Mock Exam - 3 - Solution _ (Optional).extraction.md::E343
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 17_Mock Exams / 308_Mock Exam - 3 - Solution _ (Optional).txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But that's to be expected because the deployment never told the replica set that it needs to go to three.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1226: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/17_Mock Exams/308_Mock Exam - 3 - Solution _ (Optional).extraction.md::E344
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 17_Mock Exams / 308_Mock Exam - 3 - Solution _ (Optional).txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So I think this is more interesting what we see in the deployment, which is that the deployment controllwr never did its job, it never configured it to set the replica set up to three.

- Type: Concept

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To prevent users from losing access to our application, we would like to have more than one instance or pod running at the same time.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Content: The selector section helps the replica set identify what pods fall under it, but why would you have to specify what pods fall under it if we have provided the contents of the pod definition file itself in the template.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Scheduling

### Evidence 1320: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E80
- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It's because replica set can also manage pods that were not created as part of the replica set creation.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say for example, the reports created before the creation of the replica set that match labels specified in the selector.

### Evidence 1322: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E82
- Type: Concept

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The replica set will also take those pods into consideration when creating the replicas.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1323: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E83
- Type: Comparison
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: I will elaborate this in the next slide, but before we get into that, I would like to mention that the selector is one of the major differences between replication controller and replica set.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Scheduling, Platform Engineering

### Evidence 1324: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E84
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The selector is not a required field in case of a replication controller, but it is still available when you skip it, as we did in the previous slide, it assumes it to be the same as the labels provided in the Pod definition file.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Scheduling, Platform Engineering

### Evidence 1325: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E85
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In case of replica set, a user input is required for this property, and it has to be written in the form of match labels as shown here.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1326: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E86
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The match labels selector simply matches the labels specified under it to the labels on the pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Scheduling

### Evidence 1327: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E87
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The replica set selector also provides many other options for matching labels that were not available in a replication controller, and as always, to create a replica set, run the kubectl create command, providing the definition file as input, and to see the created replicas run the kubectl get replica set command.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Scheduling, Configuration

### Evidence 1328: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E88
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To get list of pods, simply run the kube control get pods command.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1349: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E109

### Evidence 1351: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E111
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Since we are not expecting the Replicaset to create a new pod on deployment?

### Evidence 1352: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E112
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 021_Recap - ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Yes, we do, because in case one of the pods were to fail in the future, the replica set needs to create a new one to maintain the desired number of pods, and for the replica set to create a new pod, the template definition section is required.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The first is to update the number of replicas in the definition file to six, then run the kubectl apply command to specify the same file using the f parameter, and that will update the replica set to have six replicas.

### Evidence 1359: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/021_Recap - ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E119
- Type: Concept

- Content: In this lecture we will discuss about Kubernetes deployments.

### Evidence 1507: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For a minute.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1508: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let us forget about pods and replica sets and other Kubernetes concepts, and talk about how you might want to deploy your application in a production environment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1509: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say, for example, you have a web server that needs to be deployed in a production environment.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1510: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You need not one, but many such instances of the web server running for obvious reasons.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1511: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Secondly, whenever newer versions of application builds become available on the Docker registry, you would like to upgrade your Docker instances seamlessly.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1512: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, when you upgrade your instances, you do not want to upgrade all of them at once, as we just did.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1513: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This may impact users accessing our applications, so you might want to upgrade them one after the other.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1514: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And that kind of upgrade is known as rolling updates.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1515: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Suppose one of the upgrades you performed resulted in an unexpected error, and you're asked to undo the recent change You would like to be able to roll back the changes that were recently carried out.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 1516: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Finally, say, for example, you would like to make multiple changes to your environment, such as upgrading the underlying web server versions, as well as scaling your environment and also modifying the resource allocations, etc. you do not want to apply each change immediately after the command is run.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1517: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Instead, you would like to apply a path to your environment, make the changes and then resume so that all the changes are rolled out together.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1518: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: All of these capabilities are available with the Kubernetes deployments.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1519: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So far in this course, we discussed about pods, which deploy single instances of our application, such as the web application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1520: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this case, each container is encapsulated in pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1521: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Multiple such pods are deployed using replication controllers or replica sets.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1522: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And then comes deployment, which is a Kubernetes object that comes higher in the hierarchy.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1523: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 024_Recap - Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The deployment provides us with the capability to upgrade the underlying instances seamlessly using rolling updates, undo changes, and pause and resume changes as required.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments

### Evidence 1524: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E19

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1533: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/024_Recap - Deployments.extraction.md::E28

### Evidence 1676: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Before we look at how we upgrade our application, let's try to understand rollouts and versioning in a deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1677: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When you first create a deployment it triggers a rollout.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1678: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A new rollout creates a new replica set, which is recorded as a new deployment revision in the future when the application is upgraded, meaning when the container version is updated to a new one, a new rollout is triggered and a new deployment revision is created, named revision two.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1679: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This helps us keep track of the changes made to our deployment, and enables us to roll back to a previous version of deployment if necessary.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1680: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You can see the status of your rollout by running the command kube control rollout status, followed by the name of the deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1681: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To see the revisions and history of rollout, run the Kube control rollout history command followed by the deployment name, and this will show you the revisions and history of our deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1682: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There are two types of deployment strategies.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1683: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Exam Tip

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say for example you have five replicas of your web application instance deployed.

### Evidence 1684: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One way to upgrade these to a newer version is to destroy all of these, and then create newer versions of application instances.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1685: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Meaning first destroy the five running instances and then deploy five new instances of the new application version.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1686: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The problem with this, as you can imagine, is that during the period after the older versions are down and before any newer version is up, the application is down and inaccessible to users.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1687: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This strategy is known as the recreate strategy, and thankfully this is not the default deployment strategy.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

- Content: The second strategy is where we do not destroy all of them at once.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Instead, we take down the older version and bring up a newer version one by one.

### Evidence 1709: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E35

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let's look at how a deployment performs an upgrade under the hood when a new deployment is created, say, to deploy five replicas, it first creates a replica set automatically, which in turn creates the number of pods required to meet the number of replicas.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1710: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/099_Rolling Updates & Rollbacks in Deployments.extraction.md::E36

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When you upgrade your application, as we saw in the previous slide, the Kubernetes deployment object creates a new replica set under the hood and starts deploying the containers there.

- Content: Now, the problem with this approach is that during the period after the older versions are down and before any newer version is up, the application is down and inaccessible to users.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1747: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/103_Deployment Strategy - Blue Green.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 103_Deployment Strategy - Blue Green.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The second is the rolling update strategy where we do not destroy all of them at once.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1748: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/103_Deployment Strategy - Blue Green.extraction.md::E6

- Content: Instead, we take down the older version and bring up a newer version one by one.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that solves the first problem.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1814: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We now have traffic going to both the versions of applications, but it's routing it equally.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1815: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E26
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That's 50% to each deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1816: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E27
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So how do we reduce the traffic to the canary deployment?
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1817: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E28
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We only want a small percentage of traffic to go to the canary deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1818: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E29
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We can do that by simply reducing the number of pods in the canary deployment to the minimum possible, in this case one.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1819: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E30
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So since a service distributes traffic between all pods equally, together, the pods in the primary deployment gets 83% of traffic, and the single pod in the canary deployment gets 17% of traffic.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services

### Evidence 1820: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E31
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, once tests are performed and we're confident with the new version, we can now upgrade the version of pods in the primary deployment and delete the canary deployment altogether.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1821: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So one of the caveats of performing a canary update this way with just Kubernetes deployments and services is that we have limited control over the split of traffic between each deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services

### Evidence 1822: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The traffic split is always going to be governed by the number of pods present in each deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1823: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So for example, we cannot say route just 1% of traffic to the canary deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1824: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E35
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For that, we would need at least 100 pods altogether, and that's why service meshes like Istio comes with better control.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services

### Evidence 1825: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: With Istio, you can define the exact percentage of traffic to be distributed between each deployment, and it's not really dependent upon the number of pods in a deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 1826: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/06_POD Design/104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 06_POD Design / 104_Deployment Strategy - Canary.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let's say if you have just one pod in each deployment, you can still route 1% of traffic to one deployment and 99% to the other.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 2592: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: What if for some reason our application crashes and the pod fails, users will no longer be able to access our application?
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 2593: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To prevent users from losing access to our application, we would like to have more than one instance or pod running at the same time.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Observability

### Evidence 2594: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That way, if one fails, we still have our application running on the other one.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 2595: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The replication controller helps us run multiple instances of a single pod in the Kubernetes cluster, After, thus providing high availability.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Architecture

### Evidence 2596: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So does that mean you can't use a replication controller if you plan to have a single pod?
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 2597: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: No.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 2598: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Even if you have a single pod, replication controller can help by automatically bringing up a new pod when the existing one fails.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 2599: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Thus, the replication controller ensures that the specified number of pods are running at all times, even if it's just 1 or 100.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 2600: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Another reason we need replication controller is to create multiple pods to share the load across them.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 2601: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E15

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, in this simple scenario, we have a single pod serving a set of users.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 2602: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When the number of users increased, we deploy additional pod to balance the load across the two pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 2603: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E17

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If the demand further increases, and if we were to run out of resources on the first node, Snowed, we could deploy additional pods across the other nodes in the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Architecture

- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Architecture

### Evidence 2671: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E85
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments / 026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The selector is not a required field in case of a replication controller, but it is still available when you skip it, as we did in the previous slide, it assumes it to be the same as the labels provided in the Pod definition file.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Scheduling, Platform Engineering

### Evidence 2672: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E86

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In case of replica set, a user input is required for this property, and it has to be written in the form of match labels as shown here.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments

### Evidence 2673: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E87

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The match labels selector simply matches the labels specified under it to the labels on the pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Scheduling

- Content: The replica set selector also provides many other options for matching labels that were not available in a replication controller.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Scheduling

### Evidence 2709: learn-kubernetes/05_Kubernetes Concepts - Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments/026_Replication Controllers and ReplicaSets.extraction.md::E123
- Type: Exam Tip
